metadata.summary: Standardize every Python project on uv: committed uv.lock as the single source of truth, all tool calls via `uv run` to guarantee reproducible installs for AI coding agents.

content.title: uv as the Python Lockfile Floor
content.summary: Standardize every Python project on Astral's `uv` for environment management, dependency resolution, lockfile, and Python version installation. The committed `uv.lock` is the single source of truth for reproducible installs; AI coding agents must invoke commands through `uv run <cmd>` (never bare `pytest` / `python`) so the right virtualenv and the right Python version are guaranteed for every tool call.

why.p: Pre-each-command lockfile re-verification (`uv sync` / `uv run`) closes the silent-drift gap that `pip install -r requirements.txt` leaves open: an agent cannot mutate the env without uv noticing on the next call. uv is implemented in Rust and resolves ~10x faster than pip-tools or Poetry, so the cost of "always sync before run" is small enough to keep an agentic inner loop viable. After OpenAI's March 2026 acquisition of Astral, Codex bundles uv natively — the de facto Python contract between coding agents and repos in 2026.

list.item: Migrating Poetry / pip-tools / requirements.txt projects when the team can switch lockfile formats.
Multi-Python-version repos (uv installs and pins interpreter versions per project).
Any repo where AI agents will run `pytest`, type-checkers, or scripts.
Conda / scientific stacks needing CUDA, GDAL, or other binary deps from conda-forge — `pixi` handles that subset better.
Repos pinned to legacy Python (2.7) or to a private registry that uv's resolver does not understand.
Single-file scripts where a venv is more ceremony than the script is worth.
Projects that must keep `requirements.txt` for an external consumer (pull request agents, security scanners) until those consumers learn `uv.lock`.

section.name: lockfile-floor
section.p: uv produces a deterministic resolution in `uv.lock` from `pyproject.toml`. The lockfile is committed; CI installs with `--frozen` so a missing dep or a hash mismatch fails the build.

rule.statement: Commit uv.lock to the repo. Run installs with `uv sync --frozen` in CI and `uv sync` locally; mutate dependencies only via `uv add` / `uv remove` / `uv lock --upgrade`.
rule.rationale: Reproducible installs require both the manifest (pyproject.toml) and the resolved transitive set (uv.lock). Committing the lock is the only way two agents on two machines compute the same env.

rule.statement: Never invoke `pytest`, `python`, `mypy`, `ruff`, or any other project tool bare. Always wrap in `uv run <cmd>`; for global tooling, use `uv tool install` / `uv tool run`.
rule.rationale: uv re-verifies the lockfile against pyproject.toml on every `uv run`. A bare `pytest` may pick up a stale system-Python interpreter and report a false green.

example.code: uv init my-pkg --package uv add fastapi pydantic uv add --dev pytest ruff hypothesis uv lock # writes uv.lock uv run pytest -x # always via uv run uv tool install ty@latest # global tool, isolated
example.note: Lockfile is generated, env is implicit, every command runs in the project venv.

example.code: pip install -r requirements.txt pytest # which Python? which venv? pip install some-helper # not in any lockfile
example.note: No lockfile, no env contract. The next agent on a different machine gets a different result.

antipattern.reason: The lock is hash-verified; a hand edit either fails the next sync or — worse — installs an unsigned set. Use `uv lock --upgrade-package <name>` or adjust constraints in pyproject.toml and re-lock.

list.item: `uv sync` — install or update the env from pyproject.toml + uv.lock at session start.
`uv add <pkg>` / `uv add --dev <pkg>` — add a runtime / dev dependency; mutates pyproject.toml AND uv.lock atomically.
`uv remove <pkg>` — remove a dep; both files are updated.
`uv lock --upgrade` — refresh the entire lockfile to latest compatible versions.
`uv lock --upgrade-package <name>` — bump only one package, preserving the rest.
`uv run <cmd>` — run any command inside the project env; uv re-syncs first.
`uv tool install <name>` / `uv tool run <name>` — global tool isolated from project (ruff, ty, mutmut, pre-commit).

rule.statement: In CI, install with `uv sync --frozen --no-dev` (or `--all-extras` per matrix), never `uv sync --upgrade`.
rule.rationale: `--frozen` fails the build if pyproject.toml and uv.lock disagree. Without it, CI quietly relocks and you lose the auditability of the committed lockfile.

example.code: # .github/workflows/ci.yml (excerpt) - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v6 with: { enable-cache: true } - run: uv sync --frozen --all-extras - run: uv run ruff check - run: uv run pytest -x --cov=src
example.note: Frozen sync + uv run = same env locally and in CI; no drift.

antipattern.description: Mixing `pip install` calls inside a uv-managed project.
antipattern.reason: pip writes to the same venv but does not touch uv.lock. The next `uv sync` removes the package and breaks the build. Pick one tool per project.

template.purpose: Minimal PEP-621 project skeleton wired up for uv with dev-dep group.
Drop-in AGENTS.md block telling agents to use uv run and never edit uv.lock by hand.
